questions.specification: A car travels along a straight road with its velocity at time $t$ given by the function $v(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 9$, where $v$ is in meters per second and $t$ is in seconds.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Going from acceleration and velocity, to velocity and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The problem gives velocity as a function of time and asks for displacement over an interval, which is found by integrating velocity (linking velocity and displacement in kinematics).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.9—Kinematics problems > Question Type 4: Difference between distance and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Difference between distance and displacement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for total distance traveled, requiring handling sign changes of velocity (using absolute value / splitting intervals), which tests the distinction between displacement and distance.

parts.content: Find the displacement of the car from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that displacement = $\int v(t) \, dt$

- Integrate velocity function:
  - $\int (3t^2 - 12t + 9) \, dt$
  - $= t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + C$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Evaluate displacement from $t=0$ to $t=4$:
  - $s(4) = 4^3 - 6(4^2) + 9(4)$
  - $= 64 - 96 + 36$
  - $= 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $s(0) = 0^3 - 6(0^2) + 9(0) = 0$

- Total displacement = $s(4) - s(0) = 4$ meters <marks>A1</marks>

<note>All integration steps must be shown for full marks</note>

<marks>3 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To find the displacement, we must integrate the velocity function over the given time interval. This concept connects kinematics with calculus, specifically covered in **SL 5.9—Kinematics problems**.

$$s = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) \, dt$$

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
We need to integrate $v(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 9$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.

$$\text{Displacement} = \int_{0}^{4} (3t^2 - 12t + 9) \, dt$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter ∫(3x^2 - 12x + 9, 0, 4).

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9:fnInt. Enter fnInt(3X^2 - 12X + 9, X, 0, 4).

desmos.instructions: Type int(0, 4, 3t^2 - 12t + 9 dt).

calculator.description: Evaluate the integral to check the expected result.

steps.title: Find the antiderivative
steps.content:
We find the indefinite integral using the power rule formula from the **Calculus** section of the data booklet.

$$\int x^{n}dx=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$$

Applying this to each term of our function:
- $\int 3t^2 \, dt = 3\left(\frac{t^3}{3}\right) = t^3$
- $\int -12t \, dt = -12\left(\frac{t^2}{2}\right) = -6t^2$
- $\int 9 \, dt = 9t$

So, the antiderivative is:

$$[t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t]_{0}^{4}$$

steps.title: Apply Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
steps.content:
Now we substitute the upper limit ($t=4$) and lower limit ($t=0$) into our expression $s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t$ and subtract.

$$\begin{aligned} s(4) &= (4)^3 - 6(4)^2 + 9(4) \\ &= 64 - 6(16) + 36 \\ &= 64 - 96 + 36 \\ &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} s(0) &= (0)^3 - 6(0)^2 + 9(0) \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 4^3 - 6*4^2 + 9*4

ti84.instructions: Calculate 4^3 - 6*4^2 + 9*4

calculator.description: Calculate the value at the upper limit t=4

steps.title: Calculate final displacement
steps.content:
Subtracting the lower limit value from the upper limit value gives the total displacement.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Displacement} &= s(4) - s(0) \\ &= 4 - 0 \\ &= 4 \text{ meters} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Determine the total distance traveled by the car from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Find points where $v(t) = 0$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
3t^2 - 12t + 9 &= 0 \\
(3t - 3)(t - 3) &= 0 \\
t = 1 \text{ or } t &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

- Calculate positions at key times:
  - $s(0) = 0$
  - $s(1) = 1^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) = 4$
  - $s(3) = 3^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) = 0$
  - $s(4) = 4^3 - 6(4)^2 + 9(4) = 4$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Total distance = sum of absolute position changes:
  $|s(1) - s(0)| + |s(3) - s(1)| + |s(4) - s(3)|$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values:
  $|4 - 0| + |0 - 4| + |4 - 0| = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12$ meters <marks>A1</marks>

<note>All working must be shown to receive full marks</note>

<marks>3 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Identify the solution method
steps.content:
To find the **total distance** traveled, we cannot simply evaluate the definite integral of velocity from 0 to 4, as that would give displacement (net change in position). 

Instead, we must:
1. Find the times where the velocity is zero (where the car changes direction).
2. Calculate the position at these times.
3. Sum the absolute distances traveled in each time interval.

This approach calculates $\int_0^4 |v(t)| dt$ analytically.

steps.title: Find when velocity is zero
steps.content:
First, we determine when the car changes direction by setting $v(t) = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned} 3t^2 - 12t + 9 &= 0 \\ 3(t^2 - 4t + 3) &= 0 \\ 3(t - 1)(t - 3) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $t$, we find two times where the velocity is zero: $t = 1$ and $t = 3$. Both of these lie within our interval $0 \le t \le 4$, so we must calculate the position at $t=0, 1, 3, \text{ and } 4$.

gdc.instructions: In the Equation application, enter the polynomial 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0 and solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = 3X^2 - 12X + 9. Press [GRAPH]. Use [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 2:zero to find the x-intercepts.

desmos.instructions: Type the equation 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0 to see the vertical lines at x = 1 and x = 3.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the velocity function.

steps.title: Determine the position function
steps.content:
Next, we integrate the velocity function $v(t)$ to find the position function $s(t)$. We use the power rule for integration from the data booklet:

$$\int (3t^2 - 12t + 9) \, dt = \frac{3t^3}{3} - \frac{12t^2}{2} + 9t + C$$

Simplifying this gives:
$$s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t + C$$

Since we are calculating the *change* in position (distance), the constant $C$ will cancel out. For simplicity, we can let $s(0) = 0$, so $C = 0$.

gdc.instructions: Define f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x. Evaluate f(1), f(3), and f(4).

ti84.instructions: Enter Y1 = X^3 - 6X^2 + 9X. Go to the home screen and calculate Y1(1), Y1(3), and Y1(4).

desmos.instructions: Define s(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t. Type s(0), s(1), s(3), and s(4) to get the values.

calculator.description: Evaluate the position function at the key time values.

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the absolute change in position for each interval:

1.  **Interval $0 \to 1$:** $|s(1) - s(0)| = |4 - 0| = 4$
2.  **Interval $1 \to 3$:** $|s(3) - s(1)| = |0 - 4| = |-4| = 4$
3.  **Interval $3 \to 4$:** $|s(4) - s(3)| = |4 - 0| = 4$

Total Distance:

$$4 + 4 + 4 = 12 \text{ meters}$$

steps.title: Recall the distance formula
steps.content:
To find the **total distance** traveled, we must integrate the speed (the absolute value of velocity) over the given time interval. Unlike displacement, which can be zero if you return to the start, distance is always positive and cumulative.

The formula for total distance is:

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} |v(t)| \, dt $$

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
Substitute the velocity function $v(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 9$ and the time limits from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ into the distance formula.

$$ \text{Distance} = \int_{0}^{4} |3t^2 - 12t + 9| \, dt $$

Writing this integral expression is crucial for earning the method mark (**M1**) before using your calculator.

steps.title: Evaluate using GDC
steps.content:
Use the numerical integration feature on your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) to evaluate the integral directly. You must include the absolute value function, usually denoted as `abs()`, inside the integral.

This method (Topic **SL 5.11**) avoids the need to manually find where the velocity is zero and split the integral.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Run-Matrix mode
2. Press [OPTN] > [CALC] > [∫dx]
3. Press [OPTN] > [NUM] > [Abs]
4. Enter: Abs(3x^2 - 12x + 9)
5. Set limits 0 and 4
6. Press [EXE]

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] > 9:fnInt(
2. Enter limits 0 to 4
3. Inside the integral, press [MATH] > NUM > 1:abs(
4. Type the function: 3X^2 - 12X + 9
5. Add 'dX' at the end
6. Press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' for the integral symbol, then 'abs' for absolute value.

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral of the absolute value of velocity.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator yields a result of $12$.

Therefore, the total distance traveled is:

$$12 \text{ meters}$$

questions.specification: Consider the function $f(x) = x^2 + 2x + 1$ on the interval $[0, 2]$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.17—Areas under curve onto y-axis, volume of revolution (about x and y axes) > Question Type 2: Volumes of revolution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Volumes of revolution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The problem asks for the volume of a solid formed by revolving y=f(x) about the x-axis over a specified interval, which is the calculus technique of volume of revolution (disk/washer method).

parts.content: Calculate the volume of the solid formed when the graph of $y=f(x)$ is revolved about the $x$-axis on $0\le x\le 2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize the volume formula $V = \pi \int_{a}^{b} [f(x)]^2 \, dx$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Expand the integrand $(x^2 + 2x + 1)^2$ to obtain $x^4 + 4x^3 + 6x^2 + 4x + 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Set up the definite integral $\pi \int_{0}^{2} (x^4 + 4x^3 + 6x^2 + 4x + 1) \, dx$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Integrate to obtain the antiderivative $\pi \left[ \frac{x^5}{5} + x^4 + 2x^3 + 2x^2 + x \right]_{0}^{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute limits into the expression $\pi \left( \frac{32}{5} + 16 + 16 + 8 + 2 \right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the final answer $V = \frac{242\pi}{5}$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct answer with clear working shown</note>

questions.specification: Consider the function $f(x) = \sqrt{1 + x^3}$ for $x \ge 0$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.7—The second derivative > Question Type 1: Calculating the second derivatives of functions involving less rules
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Calculating the second derivatives of functions involving less rules
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly asks to find f''(x), i.e., compute a second derivative (not overly complicated).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.6—Differentiating polynomials n E Q. Chain, product and quotient rules > Question Type 3: Learning to apply chain rule on more complex composite function with possible multiple chains
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Learning to apply chain rule on more complex composite function with possible multiple chains
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) also requires f'(x) for f(x)=sqrt(1+x^3), which involves chain rule on a more complex composite (power inside a square root).

parts.content:
Use the trapezoidal rule with 4 intervals of equal width to estimate the value of the integral $I = \int_0^2 \sqrt{1 + x^3} \, dx$.
Give your answer correct to four decimal places.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine step size $h = \frac{b-a}{n} = \frac{2-0}{4} = 0.5$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate function values:
  $x_0=0, f(0)=1$
  $x_1=0.5, f(0.5)=\sqrt{1.125} \approx 1.06066$
  $x_2=1.0, f(1)=\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41421$
  $x_3=1.5, f(1.5)=\sqrt{4.375} \approx 2.09165$
  $x_4=2.0, f(2)=\sqrt{9} = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply formula: $T = \frac{0.5}{2} \left( f(0) + 2(f(0.5) + f(1) + f(1.5)) + f(2) \right)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final estimate: $T = 0.25 (1 + 9.13304 + 3) = 0.25(13.13304) \approx 3.2833$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Determine the step size
steps.content:
To use the trapezoidal rule, we first determine the width of each interval, $h$. We are integrating from $a=0$ to $b=2$ with $n=4$ intervals.

$$h = \frac{b-a}{n} = \frac{2-0}{4} = 0.5$$

steps.title: Calculate function values
steps.content:
We calculate the function values $y_i = f(x_i)$ for each point $x_i$ starting from $x_0=0$ to $x_4=2$, increasing by $h=0.5$ each step. 

The function is $f(x) = \sqrt{1 + x^3}$.

$$\begin{aligned} x_0 &= 0 & y_0 &= \sqrt{1 + 0^3} = 1 \\ x_1 &= 0.5 & y_1 &= \sqrt{1 + 0.5^3} = \sqrt{1.125} \approx 1.0607 \\ x_2 &= 1.0 & y_2 &= \sqrt{1 + 1^3} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142 \\ x_3 &= 1.5 & y_3 &= \sqrt{1 + 1.5^3} = \sqrt{4.375} \approx 2.0917 \\ x_4 &= 2.0 & y_4 &= \sqrt{1 + 2^3} = \sqrt{9} = 3 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Define f(x) and evaluate at 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2.

ti84.instructions: Enter the function into Y1, then calculate values or use the table feature.

desmos.instructions: Define f(x) and create a table for x = 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2.

steps.title: Apply trapezoidal rule formula
steps.content:
The trapezoidal rule formula is:

$$\int_a^b f(x) \, dx \approx \frac{h}{2} \left( (y_0 + y_n) + 2(y_1 + y_2 + \dots + y_{n-1}) \right)$$

Substituting our values ($h=0.5$):

$$I \approx \frac{0.5}{2} \left( (1 + 3) + 2(1.06066 + 1.41421 + 2.09165) \right)$$

steps.title: Calculate final estimate
steps.content:
Compute the sum inside the brackets and multiply to find the final estimate.

Sum of middle terms:
$$1.06066 + 1.41421 + 2.09165 = 4.56652$$

Substitute back into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} I &\approx 0.25 \left( 4 + 2(4.56652) \right) \\ I &\approx 0.25 \left( 4 + 9.13304 \right) \\ I &\approx 0.25 \left( 13.13304 \right) \\ I &\approx 3.28326\dots \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to four decimal places:
$$I \approx 3.2833$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the expression directly.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the expression directly.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the expression.

steps.title: Determine interval width
steps.content:
The trapezoidal rule estimates the area under a curve by dividing it into vertical strips and approximating each strip as a trapezoid.

First, we find the width ($h$) of each strip. The integral is from $a=0$ to $b=2$, and we need 4 intervals.

$$h = \frac{b-a}{n} = \frac{2-0}{4} = 0.5$$

steps.title: Calculate function values
steps.content:
We need the height of the function $f(x) = \sqrt{1 + x^3}$ at the boundaries of each interval: $x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0$.

Using a calculator:
- $y_0 = f(0) = \sqrt{1+0^3} = 1$
- $y_1 = f(0.5) = \sqrt{1+0.5^3} \approx 1.0607$
- $y_2 = f(1.0) = \sqrt{1+1^3} \approx 1.4142$
- $y_3 = f(1.5) = \sqrt{1+1.5^3} \approx 2.0917$
- $y_4 = f(2.0) = \sqrt{1+2^3} = 3$

gdc.instructions: Define f(x) and evaluate at specific points.

ti84.instructions: Enter the function into Y1, then use the table or value function.

desmos.instructions: Type f(x) = sqrt(1+x^3) and evaluate f(0), f(0.5), etc.

steps.title: Calculate area of first trapezoid
steps.content:
The area of a trapezoid is given by $\text{Area} = \frac{h}{2}(y_{\text{left}} + y_{\text{right}})$.

For the first strip from $x=0$ to $x=0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned} A_1 &= \frac{0.5}{2} (y_0 + y_1) \\ &= 0.25 (1 + 1.0607) \\ &\approx 0.5152 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate area of second trapezoid
steps.content:
For the second strip from $x=0.5$ to $x=1.0$:

$$\begin{aligned} A_2 &= \frac{0.5}{2} (y_1 + y_2) \\ &= 0.25 (1.0607 + 1.4142) \\ &\approx 0.6187 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate area of third trapezoid
steps.content:
For the third strip from $x=1.0$ to $x=1.5$:

$$\begin{aligned} A_3 &= \frac{0.5}{2} (y_2 + y_3) \\ &= 0.25 (1.4142 + 2.0917) \\ &\approx 0.8765 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate area of fourth trapezoid
steps.content:
For the fourth strip from $x=1.5$ to $x=2.0$:

$$\begin{aligned} A_4 &= \frac{0.5}{2} (y_3 + y_4) \\ &= 0.25 (2.0917 + 3) \\ &\approx 1.2729 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sum areas for total estimate
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the areas of the individual trapezoids to find the total estimated integral value.

$$\begin{aligned} I &\approx A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + A_4 \\ &\approx 0.5152 + 0.6187 + 0.8765 + 1.2729 \\ &= 3.2833 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Sum the areas.

ti84.instructions: Sum the areas calculated in previous steps.

desmos.instructions: Sum the areas.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The estimated value of the integral is:

$$3.2833$$

parts.content: Find $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- State first derivative: $f'(x) = \frac{1}{2}(1+x^3)^{-1/2} \cdot 3x^2 = \frac{3x^2}{2\sqrt{1+x^3}}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Differentiate again using quotient rule on $f'(x) = \frac{3x^2}{2(1+x^3)^{1/2}}$
- Let $u = 3x^2$ and $v = 2(1+x^3)^{1/2}$
- $\frac{du}{dx} = 6x$ and $\frac{dv}{dx} = 3x^2(1+x^3)^{-1/2}$
- Apply quotient rule: $f''(x) = \frac{6x \cdot 2(1+x^3)^{1/2} - 3x^2 \cdot 3x^2(1+x^3)^{-1/2}}{4(1+x^3)}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Simplify numerator by multiplying by $(1+x^3)^{1/2}$:
  Numerator $= 12x(1+x^3) - 9x^4 = 12x + 12x^4 - 9x^4 = 12x + 3x^4$
- State final second derivative: $f''(x) = \frac{3x(4+x^3)}{4(1+x^3)^{3/2}}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Prepare for differentiation
steps.content:
To find the derivative of $f(x) = \sqrt{1+x^3}$, we first rewrite the square root as a fractional exponent. This makes it easier to apply the power rule.

$$f(x) = (1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}}$$ 

This question uses calculus concepts covered in **Topic 5.6: Chain, product and quotient rules**.

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
We apply the **chain rule**. We differentiate the outer function (the power of $1/2$) and multiply it by the derivative of the inner function ($1+x^3$).

Using the power rule $$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=nx^{n-1}$$:

$$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(x) &= \frac{1}{2}(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(1+x^3) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot 3x^2 \end{aligned}$$

Rewriting this with a positive exponent gives our first answer:

$$f^{\prime}(x) = \frac{3x^2}{2\sqrt{1+x^3}}$$

steps.title: Setup the quotient rule
steps.content:
To find the second derivative $f^{\prime\prime}(x)$, we will differentiate $f^{\prime}(x)$ again. Since $f^{\prime}(x)$ is a fraction, we use the **quotient rule**:

$$y = \frac{u}{v} \Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v\frac{du}{dx} - u\frac{dv}{dx}}{v^2}$$

Let's identify our $u$ (numerator) and $v$ (denominator) from $f^{\prime}(x) = \frac{3x^2}{2(1+x^3)^{1/2}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} u &= 3x^2 \\ v &= 2(1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Differentiate u and v
steps.content:
Now we find the derivatives of $u$ and $v$ with respect to $x$.

For $u = 3x^2$:
$$\frac{du}{dx} = 6x$$

For $v = 2(1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}}$, we use the chain rule again:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dv}{dx} &= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot (3x^2) \\ &= 3x^2(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply quotient rule formula
steps.content:
Substitute these parts into the quotient rule formula:

$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = \frac{v\frac{du}{dx} - u\frac{dv}{dx}}{v^2}$$

$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = \frac{[2(1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}}] \cdot (6x) - (3x^2) \cdot [3x^2(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}}]}{(2(1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}})^2}$$

Let's simplify the denominator first:
Denominator $= 4(1+x^3)$

steps.title: Simplify the numerator
steps.content:
The numerator contains a term with a negative exponent $(1+x^3)^{-1/2}$, which acts like a fraction $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^3}}$. To simplify, we can multiply the entire numerator and denominator by $(1+x^3)^{1/2}$ to clear this fraction.

**Multiplying numerator by $(1+x^3)^{1/2}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Term 1: } & 12x(1+x^3)^{1/2} \cdot (1+x^3)^{1/2} = 12x(1+x^3) \\ \text{Term 2: } & 9x^4(1+x^3)^{-1/2} \cdot (1+x^3)^{1/2} = 9x^4 \end{aligned}$$

So the new numerator is:
$$12x(1+x^3) - 9x^4 = 12x + 12x^4 - 9x^4 = 12x + 3x^4$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 12 - 9

ti84.instructions: Calculate 12 - 9

desmos.instructions: Calculate 12 - 9

calculator.description: Verify the coefficients in the simplification: 12 - 9

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Now we combine our simplified numerator with the adjusted denominator.

Recall we multiplied the denominator $4(1+x^3)$ by $(1+x^3)^{1/2}$, so the new denominator is $4(1+x^3)^{3/2}$.

Putting it all together:
$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = \frac{12x + 3x^4}{4(1+x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$$

Factoring $3x$ from the numerator gives the final form:

$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = \frac{3x(4+x^3)}{4(1+x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$$

steps.title: Rewrite the function
steps.content:
To make differentiation easier, we first rewrite the square root function using a fractional exponent. This allows us to use the power rule.

$$f(x) = \sqrt{1 + x^3} = (1 + x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}}$$ 

This format is standard for applying the chain rule.

steps.title: Find f'(x) using Chain Rule
steps.content:
We apply the chain rule to find the first derivative. The chain rule states that for a composite function, we differentiate the outer function and multiply by the derivative of the inner function.

Using the power rule formula from the data booklet:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

Applying this:
1.  **Outer derivative:** Bring down $\frac{1}{2}$ and subtract 1 from the exponent: $\frac{1}{2}(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$
2.  **Inner derivative:** Differentiate $(1 + x^3)$ to get $3x^2$

Multiplying them together:

$$f^{\prime}(x) = \frac{1}{2}(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot (3x^2) = \frac{3}{2}x^2(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$$ 

This form is perfect for finding the second derivative using the product rule.

steps.title: Set up Product Rule for f''(x)
steps.content:
To find the second derivative $f^{\prime\prime}(x)$, we treat $f^{\prime}(x)$ as a product of two functions $u$ and $v$. This approach avoids the quotient rule.

Let:
$$u = \frac{3}{2}x^2$$
$$v = (1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$$ 

The product rule formula is:
$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = u^{\prime}v + uv^{\prime}$$

steps.title: Differentiate u and v
steps.content:
Now we find the derivatives of our two parts, $u$ and $v$.

For $u = \frac{3}{2}x^2$:
$$u^{\prime} = 2 \cdot \frac{3}{2}x = 3x$$

For $v = (1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$, we use the chain rule again:
$$v^{\prime} = -\frac{1}{2}(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{3}{2}} \cdot (3x^2) = -\frac{3}{2}x^2(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{3}{2}}$$ 

We now have all the components needed for the product rule.

steps.title: Apply Product Rule
steps.content:
Substitute $u, v, u^{\prime},$ and $v^{\prime}$ into the product rule formula $f^{\prime\prime}(x) = u^{\prime}v + uv^{\prime}$:

$$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime\prime}(x) &= (3x)(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} + \left(\frac{3}{2}x^2\right)\left(-\frac{3}{2}x^2(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{3}{2}}\right) \\ &= 3x(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} - \frac{9}{4}x^4(1 + x^3)^{-\frac{3}{2}} \end{aligned}$$ 

While this is a valid derivative, IB questions often require the answer in a simplified fractional form.

steps.title: Simplify to a single fraction
steps.content:
To combine these terms, we find a common denominator. The term with the most negative exponent determines the denominator: $(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}$. We also need a factor of 4 from the fraction $\frac{9}{4}$.

Common denominator: $4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}$

Multiply the first term by $\frac{4(1 + x^3)}{4(1 + x^3)}$ to match the denominator:

$$\begin{aligned} f''(x) &= \frac{3x \cdot 4(1 + x^3)}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} - \frac{9x^4}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ &= \frac{12x(1 + x^3) - 9x^4}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ &= \frac{12x + 12x^4 - 9x^4}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ &= \frac{12x + 3x^4}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Finally, we can factor the numerator to present the solution clearly.

Factor out $3x$ from the numerator $12x + 3x^4$:

$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = \frac{3x(4 + x^3)}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}}$$ 

So the required derivatives are:
$$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(x) &= \frac{3x^2}{2\sqrt{1 + x^3}} \\ f^{\prime\prime}(x) &= \frac{3x(4 + x^3)}{4(1 + x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Run-Matrix mode. Press OPTN -> CALC -> d/dx. Enter the function and x=1.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH -> 8 (nDeriv) to calculate the numerical derivative. Enter the function and x=1.

desmos.instructions: Type the function f(x). Then type f'(1) and f''(1) to see the values.

parts.content: Hence, determine whether the estimate in Part 1 is an overestimate or an underestimate of the exact value of $I$. Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Analyze the sign of $f''(x)$ on interval $[0, 2]$:
  Since $x \ge 0$, $3x \ge 0$, $(4+x^3) > 0$ and denominator $> 0$, so $f''(x) > 0$ for $x > 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Conclude that since the graph is concave up ($f''(x) > 0$), the trapezoids lie above the curve, so the approximation is an **overestimate** <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Strategy: Second Derivative Analysis
steps.content:
To determine whether the Trapezoidal Rule estimate is an overestimate or underestimate, we must analyze the concavity of the function $f(x)$.

- If the graph is **concave up** ($f''(x) > 0$), the trapezoids lie **above** the curve, resulting in an **overestimate**.
- If the graph is **concave down** ($f''(x) < 0$), the trapezoids lie **below** the curve, resulting in an **underestimate**.

We need to find the second derivative, $f''(x)$, and determine its sign for $x > 0$.

steps.title: Find the First Derivative
steps.content:
First, we find $f'(x)$ using the chain rule. Rewrite $f(x)$ as a power:
$$f(x) = (1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}}$$

Using the power rule $nx^{n-1}$ and chain rule:
$$f'(x) = \frac{1}{2}(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot (3x^2)$$

Simplifying this expression:
$$f'(x) = \frac{3x^2}{2\sqrt{1+x^3}}$$

steps.title: Find the Second Derivative
steps.content:
Now we find $f^{\prime\prime}(x)$ by differentiating $f^{\prime}(x)$ using the quotient rule $$(\frac{u}{v})^{\prime} = \frac{u^{\prime}v - uv^{\prime}}{v^2}$$.

Let $u = 3x^2$ and $v = 2(1+x^3)^{\frac{1}{2}}$.
Then $u^{\prime} = 6x$ and $v^{\prime} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}(1+x^3)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot 3x^2 = \frac{3x^2}{\sqrt{1+x^3}}$.

Applying the rule:
$$f^{\prime\prime}(x) = \frac{(6x)(2\sqrt{1+x^3}) - (3x^2) \cdot \frac{3x^2}{\sqrt{1+x^3}}}{(2\sqrt{1+x^3})^2}$$

steps.title: Simplify the Second Derivative
steps.content:
To simplify the numerator, find a common denominator of $\sqrt{1+x^3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} f''(x) &= \frac{12x(1+x^3) - 9x^4}{\sqrt{1+x^3}} \cdot \frac{1}{4(1+x^3)} \\ &= \frac{12x + 12x^4 - 9x^4}{4(1+x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ &= \frac{12x + 3x^4}{4(1+x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ &= \frac{3x(4+x^3)}{4(1+x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Analyze the Sign of f''(x)
steps.content:
We evaluate the sign of $f''(x)$ for the interval $x > 0$:
- The term $3x$ is **positive** ($3x > 0$).
- The term $(4+x^3)$ is **positive** ($4+x^3 > 4$).
- The denominator $4(1+x^3)^{\frac{3}{2}}$ is **positive**.

Since all components are positive:
$$f''(x) > 0 \quad \text{for all } x > 0$$

1. Go to Graph app
2. Enter f1(x) = √(1+x^3)
3. Draw graph
4. Observe the shape is concave up for x > 0

2. Enter Y1 = √(1+X^3)
3. Press [GRAPH]

2. Observe the graph is concave up in the first quadrant

calculator.description: Verify the concavity by graphing f(x) or f''(x)

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Since $f''(x) > 0$, the graph of $f(x)$ is **concave up** on the interval.

This means the straight line segments used in the Trapezoidal Rule lie **above** the curve $f(x)$. Therefore, the estimated area is greater than the exact area.

**Answer:** The estimate is an **overestimate**.

parts.content: Find the $x$-intercept of the graph of $f$.
parts.markscheme:
- Valid approach to find $x$-intercept, e.g., $3 e^{x}-6=0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $x=\ln 2$ (exact), 0.693 <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the x-intercept condition
steps.content:
To find the $x$-intercept of the graph, we need to determine the value of $x$ where the function equals zero. We set $f(x) = 0$.

$$3e^x - 6 = 0$$

steps.title: Isolate the exponential term
steps.content:
We rearrange the equation to isolate $e^x$. First, add 6 to both sides, then divide by 3.

$$\begin{aligned} 3e^x &= 6 \\ e^x &= \frac{6}{3} \\ e^x &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for x using logarithms
steps.content:
To solve for $x$ in the exponent, we take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides. This is based on the inverse relationship between exponentials and logarithms found in **Topic 1: Number and Algebra**.

$$\begin{aligned} \ln(e^x) &= \ln(2) \\ x &= \ln 2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Press [LN], type 2, then [EXE].

ti84.instructions: Press [LN], type 2, then [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'ln(2)'.

calculator.description: Calculate the natural logarithm of 2 to verify the approximate value.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the intercept, which is the exact value. The approximate value is provided for reference.

$$x = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$$

steps.title: Identify the condition
steps.content:
To find the $x$-intercept, we need to find the value of $x$ where the function crosses the $x$-axis. This occurs when $f(x) = 0$.

$$3 e^{x}-6 = 0$$

steps.title: Graph the function
steps.content:
Using your graphing display calculator (GDC), enter the function equation into the graphing menu.

$$y = 3 e^{x}-6$$

$$x \approx 0.693$$

(Note: The exact algebraic solution is $x = \ln 2$, but the calculator value is sufficient for the marks).

parts.content: Find the area of the region enclosed by the graph of $f$, the $x$-axis, and the $y$-axis.
parts.markscheme:
- Valid approach to find area, e.g., $\int_{0}^{\ln 2}\left(6 - 3 e^{x}\right) d x$ or $\left|\int_{0}^{\ln 2}\left(3 e^{x}-6\right) d x\right|$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct antiderivative: $6 x - 3 e^{x}$ or $3 e^{x}-6 x$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Correct substitution of limits: $\left(6 \ln 2 - 3 e^{\ln 2}\right) - \left(0 - 3 e^{0}\right)$ or equivalent <marks>A1</marks>
- Area $= 6 \ln 2 - 3 \approx 1.16$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Find the x-intercept
steps.content:
To determine the upper limit of integration, we need to find where the graph intersects the $x$-axis by solving $f(x) = 0$. 

$$\begin{aligned} 3e^x - 6 &= 0 \\ 3e^x &= 6 \\ e^x &= 2 \\ x &= \ln 2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Graph mode, enter y1 = 3e^x - 6. Draw the graph. Select G-Solv > Root.

ti84.instructions: Press Y=, enter Y1 = 3e^x - 6. Press GRAPH. Press 2nd > TRACE > 2:Zero. Select left bound, right bound, and guess.

desmos.instructions: Type 3e^x - 6 = 0 or graph y = 3e^x - 6 and click the x-intercept.

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
The region is enclosed by the graph, the $x$-axis, and the $y$-axis (which is the line $x=0$). From the graph and our calculation, the region lies between $x=0$ and $x=\ln 2$.

Since the graph is below the $x$-axis in this interval ($f(0) = -3$), the area is given by the integral of the upper curve ($y=0$) minus the lower curve ($y=f(x)$), or simply the absolute value of the integral.

$$ \text{Area} = \int_{0}^{\ln 2} (0 - (3e^x - 6)) \, dx = \int_{0}^{\ln 2} (6 - 3e^x) \, dx $$

highlights.text: Area of the region enclosed by

steps.title: Find the antiderivative
steps.content:
We integrate the function using the standard integration formulas from the data booklet.

Using $\int x^n \, dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$ for the constant term $6$ (which is $6x^0$) and $\int e^x \, dx = e^x$:

$$ \int (6 - 3e^x) \, dx = 6x - 3e^x $$

steps.title: Evaluate definite integral
steps.content:
Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus by substituting the limits $0$ and $\ln 2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= [6x - 3e^x]_{0}^{\ln 2} \\ &= (6(\ln 2) - 3e^{\ln 2}) - (6(0) - 3e^0) \\ &= (6\ln 2 - 3(2)) - (0 - 3(1)) \\ &= (6\ln 2 - 6) - (-3) \\ &= 6\ln 2 - 6 + 3 \\ &= 6\ln 2 - 3 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix, press OPTN > CALC > Int. Enter Int(Abs(3e^x - 6), 0, ln(2)).

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9:fnInt. Enter fnInt(abs(3e^x - 6), X, 0, ln(2)).

desmos.instructions: Type int(0, ln(2), abs(3e^x - 6))

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The exact area is $6\ln 2 - 3$. We can approximate this value:

$$\text{Area} \approx 6(0.6931) - 3 \approx 4.159 - 3 \approx 1.16$$

steps.title: Identify the region boundaries
steps.content:
To find the area enclosed by the graph, the $x$-axis, and the $y$-axis, we first need to determine the integration limits.

*   **Lower limit:** The region is bounded by the $y$-axis, which corresponds to $x = 0$.
*   **Upper limit:** The region ends where the graph crosses the $x$-axis. We need to find this $x$-intercept.

highlights.text: region enclosed by the graph of f, the x-axis, and the y-axis

steps.content:
Use your graphing calculator to find the root (zero) of the function $f(x) = 3e^x - 6$.

The $x$-intercept is at $x \approx 0.693$ (exact value is $\ln 2$). This will be our upper limit of integration.

steps.content:
Since the graph is below the $x$-axis in this region (as seen in the diagram), the definite integral of $f(x)$ will result in a negative value. To find the **area**, we must take the absolute value of the integral or subtract the function from 0.

$$ \text{Area} = \int_{0}^{0.693} \left| 3e^x - 6 \right| \, dx $$

parts.content: The region in part 2 is rotated $360^{\circ}$ about the $x$-axis. Write down an expression for the volume of the solid formed and find its value.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct expression for volume: $\pi \int_{0}^{\ln 2}\left(3 e^{x}-6\right)^{2} d x$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Attempt to expand and integrate: $\left(3 e^{x}-6\right)^{2}=9 e^{2 x}-36 e^{x}+36$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct antiderivative: $\frac{9}{2} e^{2 x}-36 e^{x}+36 x$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute limits: $\pi\left[\left(\frac{9}{2}(4)-36(2)+36 \ln 2\right)-\left(\frac{9}{2}-36+0\right)\right]$ or equivalent
- Volume $=\pi\left(36 \ln 2 - 22.5\right) \approx 7.71$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Find the upper limit
steps.content:
To find the upper limit of integration, we identify where the graph intersects the $x$-axis by setting $f(x) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 3e^x - 6 &= 0 \\ 3e^x &= 6 \\ e^x &= 2 \\ x &= \ln 2 \end{aligned}$$

The region is bounded by $x=0$ and $x=\ln 2$.

steps.title: Set up the volume integral
steps.content:
The formula for the volume of a solid of revolution about the $x$-axis is $V = \pi \int_a^b y^2 dx$. Substituting our function and limits:

$$V = \pi \int_{0}^{\ln 2} (3e^x - 6)^2 dx$$

highlights.text: Correct expression for volume: $\pi \int_{0}^{\ln 2}\left(3 e^{x}-6\right)^{2} d x$

steps.title: Expand the squared term
steps.content:
Using the **Algebraic Expansion** method, we expand the squared binomial $(3e^x - 6)^2$ into individual terms before integrating:

$$\begin{aligned} (3e^x - 6)^2 &= (3e^x)^2 - 2(3e^x)(6) + 6^2 \\ &= 9e^{2x} - 36e^x + 36 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Integrate term by term
steps.content:
Now we find the antiderivative of each term. Recall that $\int e^{kx} dx = \frac{1}{k}e^{kx}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \int (9e^{2x} - 36e^x + 36) dx &= 9\left(\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\right) - 36(e^x) + 36x \\ &= \frac{9}{2}e^{2x} - 36e^x + 36x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute limits
steps.content:
We evaluate the definite integral by substituting the limits $x=\ln 2$ and $x=0$. Note that $e^{2\ln 2} = e^{\ln 4} = 4$ and $e^{\ln 2} = 2$.

At $x = \ln 2$:
$$\frac{9}{2}(4) - 36(2) + 36(\ln 2) = 18 - 72 + 36\ln 2 = 36\ln 2 - 54$$

At $x = 0$:
$$\frac{9}{2}(1) - 36(1) + 36(0) = 4.5 - 36 = -31.5$$

steps.title: Calculate final value
steps.content:
Subtract the lower limit value from the upper limit value and multiply by $\pi$:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \pi \left[ (36\ln 2 - 54) - (-31.5) \right] \\ &= \pi (36\ln 2 - 22.5) \\ &\approx 7.71 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression directly

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly

desmos.instructions: Type the expression

steps.title: Find the integration limits
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the limits of integration. The region is rotated about the $x$-axis, bounded by the $y$-axis ($x=0$) and the point where the curve meets the $x$-axis.

Set $f(x) = 0$ to find the $x$-intercept:
$$\begin{aligned} 3e^x - 6 &= 0 \\ 3e^x &= 6 \\ e^x &= 2 \\ x &= \ln 2 \end{aligned}$$

So, our limits are $x=0$ and $x=\ln 2$.

steps.title: Set up the volume integral
steps.content:
The formula for the volume of revolution about the $x$-axis is $V = \pi \int_a^b y^2 dx$. Substituting our function and limits:

$$V = \pi \int_{0}^{\ln 2} (3e^x - 6)^2 dx$$

steps.title: Apply substitution
steps.content:
We use the substitution $u = e^x$. Differentiating with respect to $x$ gives:
$$\frac{du}{dx} = e^x = u \implies dx = \frac{1}{u} du$$

Next, we transform the limits of integration:
- Lower limit: When $x = 0$, $u = e^0 = 1$
- Upper limit: When $x = \ln 2$, $u = e^{\ln 2} = 2$

steps.title: Transform the integral
steps.content:
Substitute $u$ and $dx$ into our integral expression:

$$V = \pi \int_{1}^{2} (3u - 6)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{u} du$$

steps.title: Simplify the integrand
steps.content:
First expand the squared term $(3u-6)^2$, then divide by $u$ to prepare for integration:

$$\begin{aligned} (3u - 6)^2 &= 9u^2 - 36u + 36 \\ \frac{(3u - 6)^2}{u} &= \frac{9u^2 - 36u + 36}{u} \\ &= 9u - 36 + \frac{36}{u} \end{aligned}$$

The integral becomes:
$$V = \pi \int_{1}^{2} \left( 9u - 36 + \frac{36}{u} \right) du$$

steps.title: Perform the integration
steps.content:
Integrate each term with respect to $u$. Recall that the integral of $\frac{1}{u}$ is $\ln\left| u \right|$.

$$V = \pi \left[ \frac{9u^2}{2} - 36u + 36\ln\left| u \right| \right]_{1}^{2}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the definite integral
steps.content:
Substitute the upper and lower limits to find the value:

Upper limit ($u=2$):
$$\left(\frac{9(2)^2}{2} - 36(2) + 36\ln 2\right) = 18 - 72 + 36\ln 2 = -54 + 36\ln 2$$

Lower limit ($u=1$):
$$\left(\frac{9(1)^2}{2} - 36(1) + 36\ln 1\right) = 4.5 - 36 + 0 = -31.5$$

Subtracting the lower from the upper:
$$V = \pi ((-54 + 36\ln 2) - (-31.5)) = \pi (36\ln 2 - 22.5)$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculation screen

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a command line

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The exact volume is $\pi(36\ln 2 - 22.5)$. Calculating the approximate value:

$$V \approx \pi(24.953 - 22.5) \approx 7.71$$

The volume of the solid is approximately **7.71**.

questions.specification: Consider the function $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Integrals for total area (using absolute value)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task is to find the area between y=f(x) and the x-axis over an interval; since f(x)=x^2-4x+3 crosses the x-axis within [1,3], it requires total area (absolute value / splitting at roots).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.11—Definite integrals, areas under curve onto x-axis and areas between curves > Question Type 1: Evaluating definite integrals analytically
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Evaluating definite integrals analytically

parts.content: Find the area between the curve $y = f(x)$ and the $x$-axis from $x = 1$ to $x = 3$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Recognize** that $x = 1$ and $x = 3$ are the roots of the quadratic <marks>M1</marks>

- **Find** antiderivative: $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3} - 2x^2 + 3x$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Set up** definite integral: $\int_{1}^{3} (x^2 - 4x + 3) \, dx = F(3) - F(1)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Substitute** limits: $F(3) = 0$ and $F(1) = \frac{1}{3} - 2 + 3 = \frac{4}{3}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Calculate** value of integral: $F(3) - F(1) = 0 - \frac{4}{3} = -\frac{4}{3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** area: $\int_{1}^{3} |f(x)| \, dx = \left| -\frac{4}{3} \right| = \frac{4}{3}$ square units <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Must take absolute value of integral for final area. The graph stays on one side of the $x$-axis in the interval.</note>

steps.title: Determine limits of integration
steps.content:
First, we confirm the limits of integration by finding the roots of the quadratic function $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$. These roots represent the points where the curve intersects the $x$-axis.

Factorize the quadratic equation:
$$x^2 - 4x + 3 = (x-1)(x-3) = 0$$

The roots are $x=1$ and $x=3$. These match the interval given in the question, so we will integrate from $1$ to $3$.

steps.title: Find the antiderivative
steps.content:
To integrate $f(x)$, we find the antiderivative $F(x)$ using the power rule for integration.

Using the formula $\int x^n dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} F(x) &= \int (x^2 - 4x + 3) \, dx \\ &= \frac{x^3}{3} - 4\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right) + 3x \\ &= \frac{x^3}{3} - 2x^2 + 3x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up definite integral
steps.content:
According to the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the definite integral is calculated by evaluating the antiderivative at the upper and lower limits.

$$\int_{1}^{3} (x^2 - 4x + 3) \, dx = F(3) - F(1)$$

steps.title: Evaluate at the limits
steps.content:
Now we substitute the limits $x=3$ and $x=1$ into our antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3} - 2x^2 + 3x$.

For the upper limit ($x=3$):
$$F(3) = \frac{3^3}{3} - 2(3)^2 + 3(3) = 9 - 18 + 9 = 0$$

For the lower limit ($x=1$):
$$F(1) = \frac{1^3}{3} - 2(1)^2 + 3(1) = \frac{1}{3} - 2 + 3 = \frac{4}{3}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter calculation:

ti84.instructions: Type the expression directly:

desmos.instructions: Type in input bar:

steps.title: Calculate the integral value
steps.content:
Subtract the lower limit value from the upper limit value:

$$\int_{1}^{3} f(x) \, dx = F(3) - F(1) = 0 - \frac{4}{3} = -\frac{4}{3}$$

steps.title: Determine final area
steps.content:
The integral evaluates to a negative number because the curve lies below the $x$-axis between $x=1$ and $x=3$. Area represents a physical magnitude and must be positive, so we take the absolute value.

$$\text{Area} = \left| -\frac{4}{3} \right| = \frac{4}{3}$$

Since the curve is below the axis, the definite integral will result in a negative value. To find the geometric area, we must take the absolute value of this integral.

steps.title: Determine the final area
steps.content:
The calculator will return a value of approximately $-1.333$ (or $-\frac{4}{3}$).

The area is the magnitude (absolute value) of this result:

$$ \text{Area} = \left| \int_{1}^{3} (x^2 - 4x + 3) \, dx \right| = \left| -\frac{4}{3} \right| = \frac{4}{3} $$

questions.specification: A tank initially contains 100 liters of pure water. A solution containing salt flows into the tank at a rate of $5 e^{-t / 5}$ grams per minute, and the well-mixed solution exits at a rate of $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ grams per minute, where $x(t)$ grams is the amount of salt in the tank at time $t$ minutes $(t \geq 0)$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.18—1st order DE’s – Euler method, variables separable, integrating factor, homogeneous DE using sub y=vx > Question Type 4: Solving with the integrating factor (linear ODEs)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Solving with the integrating factor (linear ODEs)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Main task is formulating and solving a first-order linear differential equation using an integrating factor (explicitly asked to show an integrating factor and solve with x(0)=0).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.14—Implicit functions, related rates, optimisation > Question Type 4: Finding maxima and minima of a function on a closed interval
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Finding maxima and minima of a function on a closed interval
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires identifying and indicating the maximum of x(t) and when it occurs (optimization of a function on an interval).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.11—Definite integrals, areas under curve onto x-axis and areas between curves > Question Type 1: Evaluating definite integrals analytically
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Evaluating definite integrals analytically
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Total salt that has left the tank over 0≤t≤50 is found by integrating an outflow rate over time (definite integral evaluation).

parts.content: Write down the differential equation modeling the rate of change of salt in the tank, $\frac{d x}{d t}$.
parts.markscheme:
Rate in: $5 e^{-t / 5}$, rate out: $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ 
$\frac{d x}{d t}=5 e^{-t / 5}-\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

steps.title: State the Rate Balance Principle
steps.content:
To model the amount of salt $x$ in the tank, we use the **Rate Balance Principle**. This principle states that the rate of change of the salt in the tank, $\frac{dx}{dt}$, is equal to the rate at which salt enters minus the rate at which it leaves.

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \text{Rate in} - \text{Rate out}$$

steps.title: Identify the rate entering
steps.content:
We look at the question to find the rate at which salt is flowing into the tank. The question gives this value directly in grams per minute.

Rate in = $5 e^{-t / 5}$

highlights.text: flows into the tank at a rate of $5 e^{-t / 5}$ grams per minute

steps.title: Identify the rate exiting
steps.content:
Next, we identify the rate at which salt is leaving the tank. In many questions, you might need to calculate this using concentration and flow rate, but here the question explicitly gives the rate of exit in grams per minute.

Rate out = $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$

highlights.text: exits at a rate of $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ grams per minute

steps.title: Formulate the differential equation
steps.content:
Substitute the expressions for the "Rate in" and "Rate out" into our main equation:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \underbrace{5 e^{-t / 5}}_{\text{Rate in}} - \underbrace{\frac{2x}{2 t+1}}_{\text{Rate out}}$$ 

This matches the form required for **AHL 5.18** regarding first-order differential equations.

parts.content: Show that $2 t+1$ is an integrating factor for this differential equation.
parts.markscheme:
Rewrite in standard form: $\frac{d x}{d t}+\frac{2}{2 t+1}x=5 e^{-t / 5}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Integrating factor $I(t)=e^{\int \frac{2}{2 t+1} d t}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\int \frac{2}{2 t+1} d t=\ln (2 t+1)$ 
$I(t)=e^{\ln (2 t+1)}=2 t+1$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the differential equation
steps.content:
The rate of change of salt in the tank, $\frac{dx}{dt}$, is the difference between the rate of salt entering and the rate of salt leaving.

From the question:
- Rate in $= 5 e^{-t / 5}$
- Rate out $= \frac{2x}{2 t+1}$

Therefore, the differential equation is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 5 e^{-t / 5} - \frac{2x}{2 t+1}$$

highlights.text: exits at a rate of $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ grams per minute

steps.title: Rearrange into standard linear form
steps.content:
To use the integrating factor method, we must rewrite the equation in the standard linear form:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} + P(t)x = Q(t)$$

Add $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ to both sides:

$$\frac{d x}{d t} + \frac{2}{2 t+1}x = 5 e^{-t / 5}$$

steps.title: Identify P(t) and integrating factor formula
steps.content:
Identify the coefficient of $x$, which is $P(t)$:

$$P(t) = \frac{2}{2 t+1}$$

The formula for the integrating factor $I(t)$ is:

$$I(t) = e^{\int P(t) dt}$$

Substituting our $P(t)$:

$$I(t) = e^{\int \frac{2}{2 t+1} d t}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.content:
We need to compute the integral in the exponent: $\int \frac{2}{2 t+1} dt$.

Notice that the numerator ($2$) is the derivative of the denominator ($2t+1$). This is a standard integral form $\int \frac{f'(t)}{f(t)} dt = \ln|f(t)|$.

Using the rule for $\int \frac{1}{x} dx$ from the data booklet:

$$\int \frac{2}{2 t+1} dt = \ln(2t+1)$$

(Note: Since time $t \geq 0$, $2t+1$ is always positive, so we can drop the absolute value bars.)

steps.title: Simplify to find the integrating factor
steps.content:
Substitute the result of the integral back into the expression for $I(t)$:

$$I(t) = e^{\ln(2t+1)}$$

Using the logarithmic identity $e^{\ln(u)} = u$:

$$I(t) = 2t+1$$

Thus, we have shown that $2t+1$ is the integrating factor.

steps.title: Set up differential equation
steps.content:
First, we model the rate of change of salt in the tank using the given information. The rate of change $\frac{dx}{dt}$ is the difference between the rate entering and the rate leaving the tank.

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \text{Rate in} - \text{Rate out}$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 5 e^{-t / 5} - \frac{2x}{2 t+1}$$

steps.title: Rewrite in standard form
steps.content:
To prepare for the integrating factor method, we rearrange the equation into the standard linear form $\frac{dx}{dt} + P(t)x = Q(t)$ by adding the term containing $x$ to the left side.

$$\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2}{2 t+1}x = 5 e^{-t / 5}$$

steps.title: Multiply by integrating factor
steps.content:
We are asked to show that $2t+1$ is an integrating factor. Following the **Verification via Product Rule** method, we multiply the entire standard differential equation by this factor $(2t+1)$.

$$(2t+1)\left(\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2}{2 t+1}x\right) = (2t+1)\left(5 e^{-t / 5}\right)$$

Distributing the $(2t+1)$ on the left-hand side:
$$(2t+1)\frac{dx}{dt} + 2x = 5(2t+1)e^{-t / 5}$$

steps.title: Verify product rule derivative
steps.content:
Now, we check if the new left-hand side is the exact derivative of the product $x(2t+1)$. We apply the product rule $\frac{d}{dt}(uv) = u\frac{dv}{dt} + v\frac{du}{dt}$ where $u=x$ and $v=(2t+1)$.

Derivative of $v$: $\frac{d}{dt}(2t+1) = 2$

Applying the product rule:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}(x(2t+1)) &= x \cdot \frac{d}{dt}(2t+1) + (2t+1) \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} \\ &= x(2) + (2t+1)\frac{dx}{dt} \\ &= 2x + (2t+1)\frac{dx}{dt} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Comparing our result from Step 3 with the left-hand side from Step 2, we see they are identical:

$$2x + (2t+1)\frac{dx}{dt}$$ matches $$(2t+1)\frac{dx}{dt} + 2x$$

Since multiplying by $2t+1$ transforms the left side of the differential equation into the exact derivative $\frac{d}{dt}(x(2t+1))$, we have shown that **$2t+1$ is an integrating factor**.

parts.content: Solve the differential equation to find $x(t)$, given $x(0)=0$.
parts.markscheme:
Multiply by $2 t+1$: $(2 t+1) \frac{d x}{d t}+2x=5(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5}$ 
$\frac{d}{d t}[x(2 t+1)]=5(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>
Integrate: $x(2 t+1)=\int 5(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5} d t$
Use integration by parts with $u=2 t+1$, $d v=5 e^{-t / 5} d t \Rightarrow d u=2 d t, v=-25 e^{-t / 5}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$\int 5(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5} d t=-25(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5}-\int -25 e^{-t / 5}(2) d t$
$=-25(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5}+50 \int e^{-t / 5} d t$
$=-25(2 t+1) e^{-t / 5}-250 e^{-t / 5}+C$ <marks>A1</marks>
Simplify RHS: $-25 e^{-t / 5}(2 t+1+10)=-25 e^{-t / 5}(2 t+11)+C$ <marks>A1</marks>
Apply initial condition $x(0)=0$: 
$0(1) = -25 e^{0}(11) + C \Rightarrow C=275$ <marks>M1</marks>
$x(2 t+1)=275-25 e^{-t / 5}(2 t+11)$ 
$x(t)=\frac{275-25 e^{-t / 5}(2 t+11)}{2 t+1}$ or $x(t)=\frac{25\left(11-e^{-t / 5}(2 t+11)\right)}{2 t+1}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the differential equation
steps.content:
The rate of change of salt in the tank, $\frac{dx}{dt}$, is the rate of salt entering minus the rate of salt leaving.

From the question:
- Rate in $= 5 e^{-t / 5}$
- Rate out $= \frac{2x}{2 t+1}$

So, our differential equation is:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 5 e^{-t / 5} - \frac{2x}{2 t+1}$$

steps.title: Rewrite in linear form
steps.content:
To use the integrating factor method, we rearrange the equation into the standard linear form:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} + P(t)x = Q(t)$$

Adding $\frac{2x}{2t+1}$ to both sides:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2}{2t+1}x = 5 e^{-t / 5}$$

steps.title: Find the integrating factor
steps.content:
We calculate the integrating factor $I(t)$ using the formula $I(t) = e^{\int P(t) dt}$, where $P(t) = \frac{2}{2t+1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \int P(t) dt &= \int \frac{2}{2t+1} dt \\ &= \ln(2t+1) \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the integrating factor is:
$$I(t) = e^{\ln(2t+1)} = 2t+1$$

steps.title: Multiply by integrating factor
steps.content:
Multiply the entire differential equation by the integrating factor $(2t+1)$:

$$(2t+1)\frac{dx}{dt} + 2x = 5(2t+1)e^{-t/5}$$

steps.title: Prepare to integrate
steps.content:
We recognize that the left-hand side is the derivative of the product $x(2t+1)$. We can now rewrite the equation and integrate both sides with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}[x(2t+1)] = 5(2t+1)e^{-t/5}$$

$$x(2t+1) = \int 5(2t+1)e^{-t/5} dt$$

steps.title: Apply integration by parts
steps.content:
To solve $\int 5(2t+1)e^{-t/5} dt$, we use **integration by parts** with $u = 2t+1$ and $\frac{dv}{dt} = 5e^{-t/5}$.

Step 1: Differentiate $u$ and integrate $\frac{dv}{dt}$:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= 2t+1 & \frac{du}{dt} &= 2 \\ \frac{dv}{dt} &= 5e^{-t/5} & v &= \frac{5}{-1/5}e^{-t/5} = -25e^{-t/5} \end{aligned}$$

Step 2: Apply the formula $\int u \frac{dv}{dt} dt = uv - \int v \frac{du}{dt} dt$:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Integral} &= (2t+1)(-25e^{-t/5}) - \int (-25e^{-t/5})(2) dt \\ &= -25(2t+1)e^{-t/5} - \int -50e^{-t/5} dt \\ &= -25(2t+1)e^{-t/5} + 50 \int e^{-t/5} dt \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Complete the integration
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the remaining integral:

$$\begin{aligned} 50 \int e^{-t/5} dt &= 50(-5e^{-t/5}) \\ &= -250e^{-t/5} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting this back gives our general solution for the RHS:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= -25(2t+1)e^{-t/5} - 250e^{-t/5} + C \\ &= -25e^{-t/5}[(2t+1) + 10] + C \\ &= -25e^{-t/5}(2t+11) + C \end{aligned}$$

So, the equation is:
$$x(2t+1) = -25e^{-t/5}(2t+11) + C$$

steps.title: Solve for C
steps.content:
Use the initial condition $x(0)=0$ to find $C$. Substitute $t=0$ and $x=0$ into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 0(2(0)+1) &= -25e^{0}(2(0)+11) + C \\ 0 &= -25(1)(11) + C \\ 0 &= -275 + C \\ C &= 275 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Verify the arithmetic for finding C

ti84.instructions: Verify the arithmetic for finding C

desmos.instructions: Verify the arithmetic for finding C

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Substitute $C=275$ back into the equation and isolate $x(t)$:

$$x(2t+1) = 275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)$$

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1}$$

Domain $0 \le t \le 50$ shown correctly <marks>A1</marks>
Scale and labels appropriate <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the differential equation
steps.content:
First, we model the rate of change of salt in the tank using the given rates. The rate of change $\frac{dx}{dt}$ is the rate in minus the rate out.

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \text{Rate}_{in} - \text{Rate}_{out}$$

Substituting the given expressions:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 5e^{-t/5} - \frac{2x}{2t+1}$$

Rearranging this into the standard form for a linear differential equation $\frac{dx}{dt} + P(t)x = Q(t)$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2}{2t+1}x = 5e^{-t/5}$$

steps.title: Find the integrating factor
steps.content:
We calculate the integrating factor $I(t)$ using the formula $I(t) = e^{\int P(t) dt}$.

$$\begin{aligned} I(t) &= e^{\int \frac{2}{2t+1} dt} \\ &= e^{\ln(2t+1)} \\ &= 2t+1 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: $$y' + P(x)y = Q(x) \Rightarrow y I(x) = \int I(x) Q(x) dx$$

steps.title: Solve the differential equation
steps.content:
Multiply the entire differential equation by the integrating factor $(2t+1)$ and integrate both sides with respect to $t$. The left side becomes the derivative of the product $x(2t+1)$.

$$x(2t+1) = \int (2t+1) \cdot 5e^{-t/5} \, dt$$

We solve the integral on the right using **integration by parts** ($\int u \frac{dv}{dt} dt = uv - \int v \frac{du}{dt} dt$).
Let $u = 5(2t+1)$ so $\frac{du}{dt} = 10$, and $\frac{dv}{dt} = e^{-t/5}$ so $v = -5e^{-t/5}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \int 5(2t+1)e^{-t/5} dt &= 5(2t+1)(-5e^{-t/5}) - \int (-5e^{-t/5})(10) dt \\ &= -25(2t+1)e^{-t/5} + 50 \int e^{-t/5} dt \\ &= -25(2t+1)e^{-t/5} + 50(-5e^{-t/5}) \\ &= -25e^{-t/5}(2t+1) - 250e^{-t/5} \\ &= -25e^{-t/5}(2t+11) \end{aligned}$$

So the general solution is:

$$x(2t+1) = -25e^{-t/5}(2t+11) + C$$

steps.title: Find the particular solution
steps.content:
The tank initially contains pure water, so when $t=0$, $x=0$.

$$\begin{aligned} 0(2(0)+1) &= -25e^{0}(2(0)+11) + C \\ 0 &= -25(1)(11) + C \\ 0 &= -275 + C \\ C &= 275 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $C$ back into the equation gives the explicit function $x(t)$:

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1}$$

steps.title: Set up the differential equation
steps.content:
To find the function $x(t)$, we first model the rate of change of salt in the tank. The rate of change $\frac{dx}{dt}$ is the difference between the rate at which salt enters and leaves the tank.

From the question:
- Rate In = $5 e^{-t / 5}$ (g/min)
- Rate Out = $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ (g/min)

The differential equation is:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} = 5e^{-t/5} - \frac{2x}{2t+1}$$

steps.title: Find x(t) using integrating factor
steps.content:
We rearrange the equation into the standard linear form $\frac{dx}{dt} + P(t)x = Q(t)$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2}{2t+1}x = 5e^{-t/5}$$

The integrating factor is $I(t) = e^{\int \frac{2}{2t+1} dt} = e^{\ln(2t+1)} = 2t+1$. Multiplying through by this factor:

$$\frac{d}{dt}[x(2t+1)] = 5(2t+1)e^{-t/5}$$

Integrating the right side using integration by parts (where $u=2t+1, dv=5e^{-t/5}dt$) gives:
$$x(2t+1) = -25e^{-t/5}(2t+11) + C$$

Since the tank initially contains pure water, $x(0)=0$. Substituting $t=0, x=0$ gives $0 = -25(1)(11) + C$, so $C=275$. The function is:

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1}$$

highlights.text: initially contains 100 liters of pure water

steps.title: Apply Derivative Optimization
steps.content:
To find the maximum amount of salt, we use the **Derivative Optimization** approach by setting $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$. This occurs when the rate of salt entering equals the rate of salt leaving:

$$\text{Rate In} = \text{Rate Out}$$
$$5e^{-t/5} = \frac{2x}{2t+1}$$

Substituting our expression for $x(t)$ into this equation allows us to solve for $t$:

$$5e^{-t/5} = \frac{2}{2t+1} \left( \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1} \right)$$

Simplifying this equation for the calculator:
$$e^{-t/5}(4t^2+24t+111) = 110$$

2. Click the intersection with the line y=110 or find the x-intercept if set to 0

$$x(8.52) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-8.52/5}(2(8.52)+11)}{2(8.52)+1} \approx 8.17 \text{ grams}$$

parts.content: Find the exact time when the rate of decrease of salt is greatest.
parts.markscheme:
Rate of decrease $D(t) = -\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{2x}{2t+1} - 5e^{-t/5}$ <marks>M1</marks>
Find maximum of $D(t)$ (using GDC or setting $D'(t)=0$) <marks>M1</marks>
$t \approx 16.5$ minutes <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the rate of decrease function
steps.content:
First, we identify the rate of change of salt in the tank, which is the rate in minus the rate out:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \text{Rate In} - \text{Rate Out} = 5e^{-t/5} - \frac{2x}{2t+1}$$

The question asks for the **rate of decrease**. A positive rate of decrease corresponds to a negative rate of change (loss > gain). Therefore, we define the rate of decrease function $D(t)$ as:

$$D(t) = -\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{2x}{2t+1} - 5e^{-t/5}$$

highlights.text: rate of decrease of salt is greatest

steps.title: Determine the expression for x(t)
steps.content:
To graph $D(t)$, we first need the function $x(t)$. This comes from solving the linear differential equation $\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2}{2t+1}x = 5e^{-t/5}$ (likely found in a previous part of the question). Using the integrating factor method, the solution for $x(t)$ with initial condition $x(0)=0$ is:

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1}$$

highlights.text: initially contains 100 liters of pure water

steps.title: Substitute x(t) into D(t)
steps.content:
Now we substitute the expression for $x(t)$ into our rate of decrease function $D(t)$:

$$\begin{aligned} D(t) &= \frac{2}{2t+1}\left( \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1} \right) - 5e^{-t/5} \\ &= \frac{550 - 50e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{(2t+1)^2} - 5e^{-t/5} \end{aligned}$$

This is the function we will graph to find the maximum.

steps.title: Find the maximum using a GDC
steps.content:
Enter the function $D(t)$ into your graphing calculator and use the maximum finding tool to locate the peak.

We are looking for the $t$-coordinate where $D(t)$ is maximum.

3. Click on the gray dot at the peak of the curve to see the coordinates.
4. The x-coordinate represents the time t.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator shows the maximum occurs at $t \approx 16.51$ minutes.

Therefore, the rate of decrease of salt is greatest at:

$$t \approx 16.5 \text{ minutes}$$

steps.title: Recall the amount of salt x(t)
steps.content:
From previous parts of the question (solving the differential equation $\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{2x}{2t+1} = 5e^{-t/5}$), the amount of salt $x(t)$ at time $t$ is given by:

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25(2t+11)e^{-t/5}}{2t+1}$$

We will need this explicit formula to use in our calculator.

steps.title: Define the rate of decrease function
steps.content:
The question asks for the time when the **rate of decrease** is greatest. The rate of decrease, $D(t)$, is the negative of the rate of change $\frac{dx}{dt}$. It represents the net rate of salt leaving the tank.

$$D(t) = -\frac{dx}{dt} = \text{Rate Out} - \text{Rate In}$$

Substituting the rates given in the question:

$$D(t) = \frac{2x}{2t+1} - 5e^{-t/5}$$

highlights.text: rate of decrease of salt is greatest

steps.title: Differentiate to find D'(t)
steps.content:
To find the maximum rate of decrease, we must differentiate $D(t)$ with respect to time $t$. We apply the quotient rule and chain rule.

$$D'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{2x}{2t+1} - 5e^{-t/5} \right)$$

Using the derivative rule for exponentials from the data booklet:

$$D'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{2x}{2t+1} \right) - 5\left(-\frac{1}{5}e^{-t/5}\right) = \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{2x}{2t+1} \right) + e^{-t/5}$$

steps.title: Set condition for maximum
steps.content:
The maximum value of $D(t)$ occurs when its derivative is zero. Therefore, we set the condition:

$$D'(t) = 0$$

Substituting the expression for $x(t)$ into $D(t)$ allows us to define $D(t)$ solely in terms of $t$:

$$D(t) = \frac{2\left(\frac{275 - 25(2t+11)e^{-t/5}}{2t+1}\right)}{2t+1} - 5e^{-t/5}$$

Now we solve $\frac{d}{dt}(D(t)) = 0$ for $t$.

steps.title: Solve using calculator
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver or graphing features of your GDC, find the value of $t$ that maximizes $D(t)$ (or where the derivative is zero).

Plot $D(t)$ and find the maximum point, or solve $D'(t)=0$.

$$t \approx 16.5 \text{ minutes}$$

2. Type the expression for D(t): D(t) = 2*x(t)/(2t+1) - 5*e^(-t/5)
3. Click on the graph of D(t) to find the maximum point.

parts.content: Calculate the total amount of salt that has left the tank from $t=0$ to $t=50$.
parts.markscheme:
Total salt left = Total Salt In - Amount remaining at $t=50$ <marks>M1</marks>
Total In = $\int_{0}^{50} 5 e^{-t / 5} d t=[-25 e^{-t / 5}]_{0}^{50}=25(1-e^{-10}) \approx 25.0$ <marks>A1</marks>
Amount remaining $x(50)=\frac{275-25 e^{-10}(111)}{101} \approx 2.72$ <marks>A1</marks>
Total left $\approx 25.0 - 2.72 = 22.3$ grams <marks>A1 A1</marks>

steps.title: Apply conservation of mass
steps.content:
To find the total amount of salt that has left the tank, we use the conservation of mass principle. The total salt that leaves is equal to the total salt that entered minus the salt that remains in the tank at the end.

$$\text{Total Left} = \text{Total In} - \text{Salt Remaining at } t=50$$

Since the tank initially contained pure water, the initial salt was 0.

steps.title: Calculate total salt entering
steps.content:
The rate at which salt enters the tank is given by $5e^{-t/5}$. To find the total salt that entered from $t=0$ to $t=50$, we integrate this rate with respect to time.

Using the standard integral of an exponential function:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total In} &= \int_{0}^{50} 5 e^{-t / 5} \, dt \\ &= \left[ \frac{5}{-1/5} e^{-t / 5} \right]_{0}^{50} \\ &= \left[ -25 e^{-t / 5} \right]_{0}^{50} \\ &= (-25 e^{-10}) - (-25 e^{0}) \\ &= -25 e^{-10} + 25 \\ &= 25(1 - e^{-10}) \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, this value is approximately $25.0$ grams.

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter 5e^(-x/5), then limits 0 and 50.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9:fnInt. Enter the function 5*e^(-X/5), variable X, lower limit 0, upper limit 50.

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' to create an integral symbol. Set bounds 0 to 50 and enter the integrand.

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral for total salt in

steps.title: Identify salt remaining
steps.content:
We now need the amount of salt remaining in the tank at $t=50$, which is $x(50)$. Using the solution to the differential equation found in previous parts of this question:

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1}$$

Substituting $t=50$ into this expression:

$$\begin{aligned} x(50) &= \frac{275 - 25e^{-10}(2(50)+11)}{2(50)+1} \\ &= \frac{275 - 25e^{-10}(111)}{101} \\ &\approx 2.72 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression exactly as shown.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression carefully with parentheses.

desmos.instructions: Enter the fraction directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of salt remaining x(50)

steps.title: Calculate total salt left
steps.content:
Finally, we subtract the amount remaining from the total amount entered to find the total amount that left the tank.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Left} &= \text{Total In} - x(50) \\ &\approx 25.0 - 2.72 \\ &\approx 22.3 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the previously calculated x(50) from the integral result.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the previous result from 25.

desmos.instructions: Perform the subtraction.

calculator.description: Subtract amount remaining from total in

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
We need to calculate the **total amount of salt that has left the tank** over the time interval from $t=0$ to $t=50$ minutes. 

The rate at which salt leaves the tank is given by the outflow rate expression in the question specification. The total amount is the definite integral of this rate over the given time interval:

$$ \text{Total Salt Left} = \int_{0}^{50} (\text{Outflow Rate}) \, dt $$

highlights.text: total amount of salt that has left the tank from $t=0$ to $t=50$

steps.title: Determine the outflow rate expression
steps.content:
The question specifies the outflow rate as:

$$ \text{Rate}_{out} = \frac{2x}{2t+1} $$

To integrate this with respect to $t$, we need the expression for $x(t)$ (the amount of salt at time $t$). Assuming you found $x(t)$ in the previous part of the question by solving the differential equation with the initial condition $x(0)=0$, the expression is:

$$ x(t) = \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1} $$

Substituting this into the rate equation gives us the explicit function of time to integrate:

$$ \text{Rate}_{out}(t) = \frac{2}{2t+1} \left( \frac{275 - 25e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{2t+1} \right) = \frac{550 - 50e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{(2t+1)^2} $$

highlights.text: exits at a rate of $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$ grams per minute

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
Now we set up the definite integral for the total salt that has left from $t=0$ to $t=50$:

$$ \text{Total Salt Left} = \int_{0}^{50} \frac{550 - 50e^{-t/5}(2t+11)}{(2t+1)^2} \, dt $$

steps.title: Calculate using GDC
steps.content:
Since this integral is complex to solve analytically, use your graphing display calculator (GDC) to evaluate it numerically.

If you already have the expression for $x(t)$ stored in your calculator (e.g., in $Y_1$), you can simply integrate $\frac{2 \cdot Y_1}{2X+1}$. Otherwise, enter the full expression derived in Step 1.

The calculator yields a value of approximately $22.277...$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the total amount of salt that has left the tank is:

$$ \text{Total Salt Left} \approx 22.3 \text{ grams} $$

parts.content: Determine the limiting amount of salt in the tank as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
As $t \rightarrow \infty$, $e^{-t/5} \rightarrow 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
$x(t) \approx \frac{275}{2t+1} \rightarrow 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the expression for x(t)
steps.content:
From the previous part of the question, we found the expression for the amount of salt $x(t)$ at time $t$. We will use this expression to find the limit.

$$x(t) = \frac{275 - 25(2t+11)e^{-t/5}}{2t+1}$$

highlights.text: Determine the limiting amount of salt

steps.title: Analyze the exponential term
steps.content:
As $t \rightarrow \infty$, the term involving the exponential decay $e^{-t/5}$ approaches zero much faster than the polynomial term $(2t+11)$ grows. This means the entire term $25(2t+11)e^{-t/5}$ will approach zero.

$$ \lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t/5} = 0 $$

steps.title: Simplify the expression for large t
steps.content:
Since the exponential part vanishes, for very large values of $t$, the expression for $x(t)$ can be approximated by keeping only the remaining terms in the numerator:

$$x(t) \approx \frac{275 - 0}{2t+1} = \frac{275}{2t+1}$$

steps.title: Evaluate the final limit
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the limit of this simplified fraction as $t$ approaches infinity. Since the denominator $2t+1$ grows infinitely large while the numerator remains constant, the value of the fraction approaches zero.

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{275}{2t+1} = 0$$

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content: You can verify this result by graphing the function on your GDC. As you trace to larger values of $x$ (which represents time $t$), you will see the $y$-value (amount of salt) getting closer and closer to 0.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The limiting amount of salt in the tank is **0 grams**.

steps.title: Analyze the salt input rate
steps.content:
To determine the limiting amount of salt, we first analyze the rate at which salt enters the tank. The question states that the salt flows in at a rate of $5 e^{-t / 5}$. We need to see what happens to this rate as time $t$ approaches infinity.

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} 5 e^{-t / 5} = 0$$

As $t$ becomes very large, the exponent becomes a large negative number, causing the term to approach zero.

highlights.text: flows into the tank at a rate of $5 e^{-t / 5}$

steps.title: Verify input decay numerically
steps.content: We can verify this behavior using a calculator by evaluating the input rate at a large value of $t$, such as $t = 100$ minutes.

steps.title: Apply qualitative reasoning
steps.content:
The calculator shows the result is approximately $1.03 \times 10^{-8}$, which is effectively zero. 

Using the **Input Rate Analysis** approach:
1. The source of new salt ($5 e^{-t / 5}$) eventually dries up completely.
2. The solution continues to exit the tank, flushing out the existing contents.
3. Without any new salt replenishing the tank, the continuous outflow will eventually wash out all remaining salt.

Therefore, the amount of salt in the tank must approach zero.

highlights.text: solution exits at a rate of $\frac{2x}{2 t+1}$

steps.title: State the final limit
steps.content:
Based on this reasoning, the limiting amount of salt $x(t)$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$ is zero.

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = 0$$

This matches the markscheme, which awards marks for identifying that the exponential input term goes to zero and concluding the final limit.

parts.content: Find $\int\left(5-2 x^{2}\right) \,\mathrm{d}x$.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct integration: $5 x-\frac{2}{3} x^{3}+c$ <marks>A1 A1 A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award A1 for $5 x$, A1 for $-\frac{2}{3} x^{3}$, A1 for $+c$.</note>

steps.title: Separate the terms
steps.content:
To solve this indefinite integral, we can integrate each term of the expression separately.

$$ \int (5 - 2x^2) \, \mathrm{d}x = \int 5 \, \mathrm{d}x + \int -2x^2 \, \mathrm{d}x $$

steps.title: Integrate the constant term
steps.content:
First, we integrate the constant term $5$. Recall that a constant $k$ integrates to $kx$ (since $5 = 5x^0$).

$$ \int 5 \, \mathrm{d}x = 5x $$

steps.title: Apply the power rule
steps.content:
Next, we integrate the term $-2x^2$. We use the standard power rule formula from the **Calculus** section of the data booklet.

Using $\int x^n \, \mathrm{d}x = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$ with $n=2$:

$$ \int x^2 \, \mathrm{d}x = \frac{x^{2+1}}{2+1} = \frac{x^3}{3} $$

Multiplying by the constant coefficient $-2$:

$$ \int -2x^2 \, \mathrm{d}x = -2\left(\frac{x^3}{3}\right) = -\frac{2}{3}x^3 $$

steps.title: Add the constant of integration
steps.content:
Finally, we combine the integrated terms. Since this is an indefinite integral, we must add the constant of integration, usually denoted as $C$ (or $c$).

$$ 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 + C $$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The final result matches the markscheme requirements.

Answer: $$ 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 + C $$

parts.content: The function $f^{\prime}(x)=5-2 x^{2}$ and $f(0)=4$. Find $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognition that $f(x)=\int f^{\prime}(x) \,\mathrm{d}x$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute coordinates: $5(0)-\frac{2}{3}(0)^{3}+c=4$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $c=4$
- State the function: $f(x)=5 x-\frac{2}{3} x^{3}+4$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
To find the original function $f(x)$ from its derivative $f'(x)$, we need to perform indefinite integration.

$$f(x) = \int f'(x) \,\mathrm{d}x$$

Substituting the given derivative:
$$f(x) = \int (5 - 2x^2) \,\mathrm{d}x$$

steps.title: Integrate using power rule
steps.content:
We integrate the expression term by term using the power rule formula from the Calculus section of the data booklet:

$$\int x^{n}dx=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$$

For the constant term $5$ (which is $5x^0$):
$$\int 5 \,\mathrm{d}x = 5x$$

For the $x^2$ term:
$$\int -2x^2 \,\mathrm{d}x = -2\left(\frac{x^{2+1}}{2+1}\right) = -\frac{2}{3}x^3$$

Combining these gives the general solution with a constant of integration $C$:
$$f(x) = 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 + C$$

steps.title: Solve for the constant
steps.content:
To find the specific value of $C$, we use the initial condition given in the question, $f(0)=4$. Substitute $x=0$ and $f(x)=4$ into our general solution:

$$\begin{aligned} 4 &= 5(0) - \frac{2}{3}(0)^3 + C \\ 4 &= 0 - 0 + C \\ C &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final function
steps.content:
Now that we have determined $C=4$, we write the complete function:

$$f(x) = 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 + 4$$

steps.title: Use Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
steps.content:
To find $f(x)$ given the derivative $f'(x)$ and an initial condition $f(0)$, we can use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. This theorem relates the function to the definite integral of its derivative:

$$f(x) = f(0) + \int_{0}^{x} f'(t) \, \mathrm{d}t$$

steps.title: Substitute given expressions
steps.content:
We are given $f(0) = 4$ and $f'(x) = 5 - 2x^2$. Substituting these into our equation:

$$f(x) = 4 + \int_{0}^{x} (5 - 2t^2) \, \mathrm{d}t$$

steps.title: Find the antiderivative
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the integral. Using the power rule for integration $\int x^n \mathrm{d}x = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}$, we integrate each term with respect to $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} \int (5 - 2t^2) \, \mathrm{d}t &= 5t - 2\left(\frac{t^3}{3}\right) \\ &= 5t - \frac{2}{3}t^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate definite integral
steps.content:
Apply the limits of integration from $0$ to $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \left[ 5t - \frac{2}{3}t^3 \right]_{0}^{x} &= \left(5(x) - \frac{2}{3}(x)^3\right) - \left(5(0) - \frac{2}{3}(0)^3\right) \\ &= 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 - 0 \\ &= 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final function
steps.content:
Finally, add the initial value $f(0) = 4$ back into our equation for $f(x)$:

$$f(x) = 5x - \frac{2}{3}x^3 + 4$$

parts.content: Write $f(x)$ in the form $x^p$ and express $g(x)$ in the form $A+Bx^p$, where $A, B, p \in \mathbb{Q}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Express $f(x)$ as a power: $f(x)=x^2 \cdot x^{\frac{1}{2}} = x^{\frac{5}{2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Express $g(x)$ using fractional power: $g(x)=4-2 x^{\frac{5}{2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert radical to exponent
steps.content:
To write $f(x)$ in the form $x^p$, we first convert the square root term into a fractional exponent using the rule $\sqrt{x} = x^{\frac{1}{2}}$.

Substituting this into the expression for $f(x)$:

$$f(x) = x^2 \cdot x^{\frac{1}{2}}$$ 

This uses the **Laws of Exponents** covered in **Topic 1: Number and Algebra**.

steps.title: Apply addition law of indices
steps.content:
Next, we use the multiplication law for indices: $$x^a \cdot x^b = x^{a+b}$$

Adding the exponents $2$ and $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$2 + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{4}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{5}{2}$$

Therefore, the simplified form is:

$$f(x) = x^{\frac{5}{2}}$$ 

This gives us the value $p = \frac{5}{2}$.
